sentences.chinese: 幽、厲之後，王道缺，禮樂衰，孔子修舊起廢，論《詩》、《書》，作《春秋》，則學者至今則之。
sentences.literal: After You and Li, the kingly way was lacking, rites and music declined; Confucius repaired the old and raised the fallen, discussed the Odes and Documents, composed the Spring and Autumn, and scholars to the present take it as their model.
sentences.idiomatic: After Kings You and Li the royal way cracked and ritual music faded; Confucius restored the broken canon, taught the Odes and Documents, and wrote the Spring and Autumn—still the scholar's polestar.

sentences.chinese: 自獲麟以來四百有餘歲，而諸侯相兼，史記放絕。
sentences.literal: From the capture of the unicorn onward over four hundred years, while the feudal lords swallowed one another, scribal records were scattered and cut off.
sentences.idiomatic: More than four hundred years have passed since the unicorn hunt, ages of annexation among the lords, and the annals lie in ruins.

sentences.chinese: 今漢興，海內一統，明主賢君，忠臣義士，予為太史而不論載，廢天下之文，予甚懼焉，爾其念哉！」
sentences.literal: Now Han has risen, within the seas unified, enlightened lords and worthy kings, loyal ministers and righteous knights—if I as grand scribe do not discuss and record them, I discard the writings of all under heaven; I am greatly afraid of this—you must heed it!"
sentences.idiomatic: Han now rules a single realm—clear-sighted sovereigns, loyal ministers, gallant men—yet if I, grand scribe, leave them unwritten I would silence the age itself; I dread that—remember!"

sentences.chinese: 遷俯首流涕曰：「小子不敏，請悉論先人所次舊聞，不敢闕。」
sentences.literal: Qian bowed his head, weeping, and said, "Your young servant is not keen; I beg to discuss in full what my forbears arranged of old hearsay—I dare not omit."
sentences.idiomatic: Qian bowed in tears: "I am dull, yet I will set down everything my father ordered from tradition—nothing shall be left out."

sentences.chinese: 卒三歲，而遷為太史令，紬史記石室金鐀之書。
sentences.literal: Three years after he died, Qian became grand scribe director and drew out from the stone chamber and metal-chested books the scribal records.
sentences.idiomatic: Three years later Qian was appointed grand scribe director and began combing the archives locked in stone vaults and metal chests.

sentences.chinese: 五年而當太初元年，十一月甲子朔旦冬至，天歷始改，建於明堂，諸神受記。
sentences.literal: In the fifth year it fell on the first year of Taichu; in the eleventh month, jiazi new moon at dawn winter's midpoint, heaven's calendar began to change; it was established in the Bright Hall, and the host of spirits received their registers.
sentences.idiomatic: Five years on came Taichu 1: the winter solstice dawned on a jiazi new moon in the eleventh month, the calendar was reformed, orders went out from the Bright Hall, and the gods received their rosters.

sentences.chinese: 太史公曰：「先人有言「『自周公卒五百歲而有孔子，孔子至於今五百歲，有能紹而明之，正《易傳》，繼《春秋》，本《詩》、《書》、《禮》、《樂》之際。』
sentences.literal: The grand scribe said, "The ancestors had a saying: 'From the Duke of Zhou's death five hundred years and then there was Confucius; from Confucius to now five hundred years—should there be one able to continue and clarify it, rectify the Commentary on the Changes, succeed the Spring and Autumn, and root it in the juncture of the Odes, Documents, Rites, and Music.'"
sentences.idiomatic: The grand scribe said: "Our forebears held that five centuries after the Duke of Zhou came Confucius, and five centuries after Confucius someone must carry the torch—rectifying the Changes commentary, continuing the Spring and Autumn, grounding it in the Odes, Documents, Rites, and Music."

sentences.chinese: 意在斯乎！
sentences.literal: The meaning lies herein!
sentences.idiomatic: The mandate lies here!

sentences.chinese: 意在斯乎！
sentences.literal: The meaning lies herein!
sentences.idiomatic: Is not the burden here?

sentences.chinese: 小子何敢攘焉！」
sentences.literal: How should a young man dare usurp that!"
sentences.idiomatic: How dare a junior like me claim that task!"

sentences.chinese: 上大夫壺遂曰：「昔孔子為何作《春秋》哉？」
sentences.literal: Senior grandee Hu Sui said, "In old days why did Confucius compose the Spring and Autumn?"
sentences.idiomatic: Hu Sui asked: "Why did Confucius write the Spring and Autumn?"

sentences.chinese: 太史公曰：「余聞之董生：『周道廢，孔子為魯司寇，諸侯害之，大夫壅之。
sentences.literal: The grand scribe said, "I heard it from Master Dong: 'Zhou's way was cast aside; Confucius became Lu's minister of crime; the feudal lords harmed him, great officers blocked him.
sentences.idiomatic: The grand scribe answered: "Dong Zhongshu told me: 'When the Zhou way collapsed, Confucius took office in Lu; the lords feared him, ministers hemmed him in.'"

sentences.chinese: 孔子知時之不用，道之不行也，是非二百四十二年之中，以為天下儀表，貶諸侯，討大夫，以達王事而已矣。』
sentences.literal: Confucius knew the times would not employ him, the way would not be practiced; he judged right and wrong within two hundred forty-two years, took it as the model for all under heaven, demoted feudal lords, censured great officers, only to bring kingly affairs to light.'"
sentences.idiomatic: Knowing his age would not use him, he read two hundred forty-two years of history as a mirror for the realm—cutting down arrogant lords and ministers to reveal the kingly thread.'"

sentences.chinese: 子曰：『我欲載之空言，不如見之於行事之深切著明也。』
sentences.literal: The Master said, 'I wished to set it forth in empty words, but it is not as good as seeing it in deeds, deep, cutting, and clear.'"
sentences.idiomatic: As the Master put it, he would rather show the point in deeds than preach it in hollow words."

sentences.chinese: 《春秋》上明三王之道，下辨人事之經紀，別嫌疑，明是非，定猶與，善善惡惡，賢賢賤不肖，存亡國，繼絕世，補弊起廢，王道之大者也。
sentences.literal: The Spring and Autumn above clarifies the way of the three kings, below distinguishes the warp and woof of human affairs, separates what is doubtful, clarifies right and wrong, settles hesitation, esteems the good and hates the bad, honors the worthy and demeans the unworthy, preserves perishing states, continues cut-off lines, mends decay and raises the fallen—the greatness of the kingly way lies herein.
sentences.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn lights the way of the ancient kings above and sorts human affairs below—sorting doubt from certainty, right from wrong, praising good and condemning evil, exalting worth and shaming baseness, saving doomed states and broken lines, healing what was broken: that is the kingly way in full.

sentences.chinese: 《易》，著天地、陰陽、四時、五行，故長於變；
sentences.literal: The Changes manifests heaven and earth, Yin and Yang, four seasons, and five phases; therefore it excels at change;
sentences.idiomatic: The Changes maps heaven, earth, yin and yang, seasons, and phases—so it masters transformation;

sentences.chinese: 《禮》，綱紀人倫，故長於行；
sentences.literal: The Rites cords and net human relations; therefore it excels at conduct;
sentences.idiomatic: The Rites bind human relations—so it governs conduct;

sentences.chinese: 《書》，記先王之事，故長於政；
sentences.literal: The Documents records former kings' affairs; therefore it excels at government;
sentences.idiomatic: The Documents records the deeds of past kings—so it serves statecraft;

sentences.chinese: 《詩》，記山川、溪谷、禽獸、草木、牝牡、雌雄，故長於風；
sentences.literal: The Odes records mountains and rivers, streams and valleys, birds and beasts, plants and trees, female and male; therefore it excels in the airs;
sentences.idiomatic: The Odes paints landscape and creature, male and female—so it catches the folk airs;

sentences.chinese: 《樂》，樂所以立，故長於和；
sentences.literal: The Music is what music relies on to stand; therefore it excels at harmony;
sentences.idiomatic: The Music shows what harmony rests on—so it cultivates concord;

sentences.chinese: 《春秋》，辯是非，故長於治人。
sentences.literal: The Spring and Autumn distinguishes right and wrong; therefore it excels at governing men.
sentences.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn judges right and wrong—so it teaches how to rule men.

sentences.chinese: 是故《禮》以節人，《樂》以發和，《書》以道事，《詩》以達意，《易》以道化，《春秋》以道義。
sentences.literal: Therefore the Rites by them restrain men, the Music by them issues harmony, the Documents by them convey affairs, the Odes by them express intent, the Changes by them convey transformation, the Spring and Autumn by them convey duty.
sentences.idiomatic: Thus the Rites curb conduct, the Music releases harmony, the Documents narrate policy, the Odes voice feeling, the Changes speak of change, and the Spring and Autumn speaks of moral duty.

sentences.chinese: 撥亂世反之正，莫近於《春秋》。
sentences.literal: To set aright a chaotic age and return it to correctness, nothing comes nearer than the Spring and Autumn.
sentences.idiomatic: Nothing brings a crooked age back to the straight like the Spring and Autumn.

sentences.chinese: 《春秋》文成數萬，其指數千。
sentences.literal: The Spring and Autumn's text forms several myriads of graphs, its import several thousand.
sentences.idiomatic: The text runs to tens of thousands of characters, its meanings to thousands.

sentences.chinese: 萬物之散聚皆在《春秋》。
sentences.literal: The myriad things' scattering and gathering are all in the Spring and Autumn.
sentences.idiomatic: Every rise and fall of things lies folded in the Spring and Autumn.

sentences.chinese: 《春秋》之中，弒君三十六，亡國五十二，諸侯奔走不得保社稷者不可勝數。
sentences.literal: Within the Spring and Autumn, rulers murdered were thirty-six, states lost fifty-two, feudal lords who fled in disorder and could not preserve altars of soil and grain were beyond counting.
sentences.idiomatic: It records thirty-six regicides, fifty-two fallen states, and countless lords who fled and lost their altars.

sentences.chinese: 察其所以，皆失其本已。
sentences.literal: Examining the reasons for this, all had lost their root.
sentences.idiomatic: Trace each disaster and you find the root neglected.

sentences.chinese: 故《易》曰『差以豪氂，謬以千里』。
sentences.literal: Therefore the Changes says, 'Err by a hair's breadth, miss by a thousand li.'
sentences.idiomatic: Hence the Changes: "A hair's-width error opens a thousand li of wrong."

sentences.chinese: 故『臣弒君，子弒父，非一朝一夕之故，其漸久矣』。
sentences.literal: Therefore 'ministers murdering lords, sons murdering fathers—these are not the work of one morning or evening; their gradual growth has been long.'
sentences.idiomatic: Hence "regicide and parricide do not spring up overnight—they creep in over long years."

sentences.chinese: 有國者不可以不知《春秋》，前有讒而不見，後有賊而不知。
sentences.literal: Those who possess a state cannot fail to know the Spring and Autumn; ahead they have slanderers yet do not see them, behind they have traitors yet do not know them.
sentences.idiomatic: No ruler can afford to ignore the Spring and Autumn: without it he misses slander in front and treachery behind.

sentences.chinese: 為人臣者不可以不知《春秋》，守經事而不知其宜，遭變事而不知其權。
sentences.literal: Those who are ministers cannot fail to know the Spring and Autumn; guarding routine affairs yet not knowing what is fitting, meeting changed affairs yet not knowing what is expedient.
sentences.idiomatic: No minister can ignore it: in calm times he will misjudge what is right, in crisis he will miss the proper expedient.

sentences.chinese: 為人君父者而不通於《春秋》之義者，必蒙首惡之名。
sentences.literal: Those who are lords and fathers yet do not penetrate the Spring and Autumn's meanings will surely bear the name of chief evildoer.
sentences.idiomatic: A father-ruler who does not grasp its lessons wears the brand of arch-villain.

sentences.chinese: 為人臣子不通於《春秋》之義者，必陷篡弒誅死之罪。
sentences.literal: Those who are ministers and sons yet do not penetrate the Spring and Autumn's meanings will surely fall into the crimes of usurpation, regicide, execution, and death.
sentences.idiomatic: Sons and subjects who miss its point invite charges of usurpation, murder, and capital crime.

sentences.chinese: 其實皆為善為之，而不知其義，被之空言不敢辭。
sentences.literal: In fact they all acted intending good, yet did not know its meaning; bearing empty reproach they dare not decline.
sentences.idiomatic: They often meant well but missed the principle, then could not escape the verdict of history.

sentences.chinese: 夫不通禮義之指，至於君不君，臣不臣，父不父，子不子。
sentences.literal: As for not penetrating the point of ritual and duty, it reaches the point where the lord is no lord, the minister no minister, the father no father, the son no son.
sentences.idiomatic: Without ritual and duty, lords cease to rule, ministers to serve, fathers to guide, sons to obey.

sentences.chinese: 夫君不君則犯，臣不臣則誅，父不父則無道，子不子則不孝：此四行者，天下之大過也。
sentences.literal: As for the lord not being lord, then he is violated; the minister not being minister, then he is executed; the father not being father, then there is no way; the son not being son, then there is no filial piety: these four conducts are the great faults of all under heaven.
sentences.idiomatic: A false lord is attacked, a false minister killed, a false father is lawless, a false son unfilial—these are the worst crimes under heaven.

sentences.chinese: 以天下大過予之，受而不敢辭。
sentences.literal: To apply to them the great faults of all under heaven—they receive them and dare not decline.
sentences.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn pins those great faults on them, and they cannot shrug the name off.

sentences.chinese: 故《春秋》者，禮義之大宗也。
sentences.literal: Therefore the Spring and Autumn is the great lineage of ritual and duty.
sentences.idiomatic: Thus the Spring and Autumn is the great charter of ritual and right.

sentences.chinese: 夫禮禁未然之前，法施已然之後；
sentences.literal: As for ritual, it forbids before it has happened; as for law, it applies after it has already happened;
sentences.idiomatic: Ritual checks evil before the fact; law punishes after the fact;

sentences.chinese: 法之所為用者易見，而禮之所為禁者難知。」
sentences.literal: what law does in being applied is easy to see, while what ritual does in forbidding is hard to know."
sentences.idiomatic: law's work is plain to the eye, ritual's prevention is subtle."

sentences.chinese: 壺遂曰：「孔子之時，上無明君，下不得任用，故作《春秋》，垂空文以斷禮義，當一王之法。
sentences.literal: Hu Sui said, "In Confucius's time, above there was no enlightened lord, below he could not obtain employment; therefore he composed the Spring and Autumn, hung empty text to judge ritual and duty, and matched the laws of one king.
sentences.idiomatic: Hu Sui said: "Confucius had no worthy ruler above and no office below, so he wrote the Spring and Autumn—pure text that would stand as kingly law for ritual and right."

sentences.chinese: 今夫子上遇明天子，下得守職，萬事既具，咸各序其宜，夫子所論，欲以何明？」
sentences.literal: Now as for you, sir, above you meet an enlightened Son of Heaven, below you obtain to keep your post; the myriad affairs are already complete, all each ordered in their fitness—what is it your discourse wishes to clarify?"
sentences.idiomatic: You serve a brilliant sovereign and hold a steady post; every office runs as it should—what, then, do you mean to illuminate?"

sentences.chinese: 太史公曰：「唯唯，否否，不然。
sentences.literal: The grand scribe said, "Yes yes, no no—it is not so.
sentences.idiomatic: The grand scribe said: "Yes and no—not quite."

sentences.chinese: 余聞之先人曰：『虙戲至純厚，作《易》八卦。
sentences.literal: I heard it from my forbears: 'Fuxi was utmost pure and thick; he made the Changes' eight trigrams.
sentences.idiomatic: My father used to say that Fuxi, in utter simplicity, first drew the eight trigrams of the Changes.

sentences.chinese: 堯、舜之盛，《尚書》載之，禮樂作焉。
sentences.literal: Yao and Shun's splendor, the Documents records it; rites and music were made therefrom.
sentences.idiomatic: The glory of Yao and Shun fills the Documents, and ritual and music grew from that age.

sentences.chinese: 湯、武之降，詩人歌之。
sentences.literal: Tang and Wu's decline, the poets sang it.
sentences.idiomatic: The poets sang the rise and turn of Tang and Wu.

sentences.chinese: 《春秋》采善貶惡，推三代之德，褒周室，非獨刺譏而已也。』
sentences.literal: The Spring and Autumn gathers good and demotes evil, extends the virtue of the three ages, praises the Zhou house—it is not only censure and ridicule.'
sentences.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn praises good, blames evil, carries the three dynasties' virtue forward, and honors Zhou—it is more than satire alone.

sentences.chinese: 漢興已來，至明天子，獲符瑞，封禪，改正朔，易服色，受命於穆清，澤流罔極，海外殊俗，重譯款塞，請來獻見者，不可勝道。
sentences.literal: From Han's rise to the enlightened Son of Heaven, obtaining tallies and omens, feng and shan, changing the first month of the year, altering dress colors, receiving the mandate in vast purity, bounty flowing without limit, beyond the seas strange customs, double-interpreted coming to the passes, requesting to come present audience—cannot be fully told.
sentences.idiomatic: Since Han rose down to our enlightened sovereign—omens gathered, feng and shan performed, calendar and vestments reformed, the mandate received in silent heaven, grace flooding without end, foreign peoples thronging the frontier with tribute beyond counting—

sentences.chinese: 臣下百官，力誦聖德，猶不能宣盡其意。
sentences.literal: Ministers below and the hundred offices, striving to chant the sage virtue, still cannot declare and exhaust its meaning.
sentences.idiomatic: the hundred officials strain to hymn his holiness yet cannot exhaust it.

sentences.chinese: 且士賢能矣，而不用，有國者恥也；
sentences.literal: Moreover when a knight is worthy and able yet is not employed, it is the shame of him who possesses a state;
sentences.idiomatic: To leave a worthy man unused shames the throne;

sentences.chinese: 主上明聖，德不布聞，有司之過也。
sentences.literal: when the lord above is enlightened and sage yet his virtue is not spread and heard, it is the fault of those in charge.
sentences.idiomatic: if a sage ruler's virtue goes unreported, the officials have failed.

sentences.chinese: 且余掌其官，廢明聖盛德不載，滅功臣、賢大夫之業不述，墮先人所言，罪莫大焉。
sentences.literal: Moreover I grasp its office; to cast aside enlightened sage splendor and not record it, to extinguish merit ministers' and worthy great officers' undertakings and not narrate them, to ruin what forbears spoke of—no crime is greater.
sentences.idiomatic: I hold this office: if I let sage brilliance go unwritten, let the deeds of heroes and good ministers fade, I betray my father's charge—there is no greater crime.

sentences.chinese: 余所謂述故事，整齊其世傳，非所謂作也，而君比之《春秋》，謬矣。」
sentences.literal: What I call narrating old affairs, ordering their transmitted generations, is not what is called making; yet you, sir, compare it to the Spring and Autumn—wrong."
sentences.idiomatic: I only arrange old stories and set chronicles in order—I am not "making" a classic as Confucius did; to liken my work to the Spring and Autumn is mistaken."

sentences.chinese: 於是論次其文。
sentences.literal: Thereupon he discussed and ordered his text.
sentences.idiomatic: He then set his manuscript in order.

sentences.chinese: 十年而遭李陵之禍，幽於累紲。
sentences.literal: In the tenth year he met with Li Ling's disaster and was imprisoned in fetters.
sentences.idiomatic: Ten years in came the Li Ling affair and prison chains.

sentences.chinese: 乃喟然而歎曰：「是余之罪夫！
sentences.literal: He then sighed deeply and said, "This is my crime, is it not!
sentences.idiomatic: He sighed, "My own fault!

sentences.chinese: 身虧不用矣。」
sentences.literal: The body is marred—there is no more employment."
sentences.idiomatic: My body is ruined—useless now."

sentences.chinese: 退而深惟曰：「夫《詩》、《書》隱約者，欲遂其志之思也。」
sentences.literal: He withdrew and thought deeply, saying, "As for the Odes and Documents being cryptic, it is the thought of wishing to fulfill one's intent."
sentences.idiomatic: Brooding alone he said, "The Odes and Documents speak in veiled words because their authors meant to voice what they could not say outright."

sentences.chinese: 卒述陶唐以來，至於麟止，自黃帝始。
sentences.literal: In the end he narrated from Pottery Tang down to stopping at the unicorn, beginning from the Yellow Thearch.
sentences.idiomatic: He carried the narrative from the Yellow Thearch through Yao's line down to the capture of the unicorn.

sentences.chinese: 《五帝本紀》第一，《夏本紀》第二，《殷本紀》第三，《周本紀》第四，《秦本紀》第五，《始皇本紀》第六，《項羽本紀》第七，《高祖本紀》第八，《呂後本紀》第九，《孝文本紀》第十，《孝景本紀》第十一，《今上本紀》第十二。
sentences.literal: "Basic Annals of the Five Thearchs" first; "Basic Annals of Xia" second; "Basic Annals of Yin" third; "Basic Annals of Zhou" fourth; "Basic Annals of Qin" fifth; "Basic Annals of the First Emperor" sixth; "Basic Annals of Xiang Yu" seventh; "Basic Annals of Gaozu" eighth; "Basic Annals of Empress Lü" ninth; "Basic Annals of Emperor Xiaowen" tenth; "Basic Annals of Emperor Xiaojing" eleventh; "Basic Annals of the Reigning Sovereign" twelfth.
sentences.idiomatic: Twelve basic annals: from the Five Thearchs through Xia, Yin, Zhou, Qin, the First Emperor, Xiang Yu, Gaozu, Empress Lü, Emperors Wen and Jing, to the reigning emperor.

sentences.chinese: 《三代世表》第一，《十二諸侯年表》第二，《六國年表》第三，《秦楚之際月表》第四，《漢諸侯年表》第五，《高祖功臣年表》第六，《惠景間功臣年表》第七，《建元以來侯者年表》第八，《王子侯者年表》第九，《漢興以來將相名臣年表》第十。
sentences.literal: "Genealogical Table of the Three Ages" first; "Yearly Table of the Twelve Feudal Lords" second; "Yearly Table of the Six States" third; "Monthly Table of the Qin-Chu Interregnum" fourth; "Yearly Table of Han Feudal Lords" fifth; "Yearly Table of Gaozu's Merit Ministers" sixth; "Yearly Table of Merit Ministers between Hui and Jing" seventh; "Yearly Table of Marquises since Jianyuan" eighth; "Yearly Table of Princely Marquises" ninth; "Yearly Table of Generals, Ministers, and Noted Officials since Han's Rise" tenth.
sentences.idiomatic: Ten tables: royal genealogy, the twelve lords, the six states, the Qin-Chu interlude month by month, Han feudatories, Gaozu's ministers, the Hui-Jing ministers, marquises since Jianyuan, princely marquises, and Han's generals and ministers.

sentences.chinese: 《禮書》第一，《樂書》第二，《律書》第三，《歷書》第四，《天官書》第五，《封禪書》第六，《河渠書》第七，《平准書》第八。
sentences.literal: "Treatise on Rites" first; "Treatise on Music" second; "Treatise on Pitch-pipes" third; "Treatise on the Calendar" fourth; "Treatise on Celestial Offices" fifth; "Treatise on the Feng and Shan Sacrifices" sixth; "Treatise on Rivers and Canals" seventh; "Treatise on the Balanced Standard" eighth.
sentences.idiomatic: Eight treatises: rites, music, pitch and law, calendrics, astronomy, the feng and shan rites, hydraulics, and state finance.

sentences.chinese: 《吳太伯世家》第一，《齊太公世家》第二，《魯周公世家》第三，《燕召公世家》第四，《管蔡世家》第五，《陳杞世家》第六，《衛康叔世家》第七，《宋微子世家》第八，《晉世家》第九，《楚世家》第十，《越世家》第十一，《鄭世家》第十二，《趙世家》第十三，《魏世家》第十四，《韓世家》第十五，《田完世家》第十六，《孔子世家》第十七，《陳涉世家》第十八，《外戚世家》第十九，《楚元王世家》第二十，《荊燕王世家》第二十一，《齊悼惠王世家》第二十二，《蕭相國世家》第二十三，《曹相國世家》第二十四，《留侯世家》第二十五，《陳丞相世家》第二十六，《絳侯世家》第二十七，《梁孝王世家》第二十八，《五宗世家》第二十九，《三王世家》第三十。
sentences.literal: He lists the thirty hereditary houses in order from Wu Taibo through the Three Kings, covering the great Zhou feudatories, Warring States houses, Confucius, Chen She, Han imperial affines and princes, and founding ministers such as Xiao He and Cao Shen.
sentences.idiomatic: Thirty hereditary houses run from Wu Taibo through the great feudal houses of Zhou and Warring States down to Han princes and ministers of state.

sentences.chinese: 《伯夷列傳》經一，《管晏列傳》第二，《老子韓非列傳》第三，《司與穰苴列傳》第四，《孫子吳起列傳》第五，《伍子胥列傳》第六，《仲尼弟子列傳》第七，《商君列傳》第八，《蘇秦列傳》第九，《張儀列傳》第十，《樗裡甘茂列傳》第十一，《穰侯列傳》第十二，《白起王翦列傳》第十三，《孟子荀卿列傳》第十四，《平原虞卿列傳》第十五，《孟嘗君列傳》第十六，《魏公子列傳》第十七，《春申君列傳》第十八，《范睢蔡澤列傳》第十九，《樂毅列傳》第二十，《廉頗藺相如列傳》第二十一，《田單列傳》第二十二，《魯仲連列傳》第二十三，《屈原賈生列傳》第二十四，《呂不韋列傳》第二十五，《刺客列傳》第二十六，《李斯列傳》第二十七，《蒙恬列傳》第二十八，《張耳陳餘列傳》第二十九，《魏豹彭越列傳》第三十，《黥布列傳》第三十一，《淮陰侯韓信列傳》第三十二，《韓王信盧綰列傳》第三十三，《田儋列傳》第三十四，《樊酈滕灌列傳》第三十五，《張丞相倉列傳》第三十六，《酈生陸賈列傳》第三十七，《傅靳崩阜成侯列傳》第三十八，《劉敬叔孫通列傳》第三十九，《季布欒布列傳》第四十，《爰盎朝錯列傳》第四十一，《張釋之馮唐列傳》第四十二，《萬石張叔列傳》第四十三，《田叔列傳》第四十四，《扁鵲倉公列傳》第四十五，《吳王濞列傳》第四十六，《魏其武安列傳》第四十七，《韓長孺列傳》第四十八，《李將軍列傳》第四十九，《衛將軍驃騎列傳》第五十，《平津主父列傳》第五十一，《匈奴列傳》第五十二，《南越列傳》第五十三，《閩越列傳》第五十四，《朝鮮列傳》第五十五，《西南夷列傳》第五十六，《司馬相如列傳》第五十七，《淮南衡山列傳》第五十八，《循吏列傳》第五十九，《汲鄭列傳》第六十，《儒林列傳》第六十一，《酷吏列傳》第六十二，《大宛列傳》第六十三，《遊俠列傳》第六十四，《佞幸列傳》第六十五，《滑稽列傳》第六十六，《日者列傳》第六十七，《龜策列傳》第六十八，《貨殖列傳》第六十九。
sentences.literal: He enumerates the sixty-nine biographies from Bo Yi through Commodity Producers (the received text has 經 for 第一 and 司與 for 司馬 in two titles).
sentences.idiomatic: Sixty-nine biographies march from Bo Yi and Shu Qi through classical philosophers, Warring States strategists and lords, Qin ministers, Chu-Han contenders, early Han ministers and generals, frontier peoples, and ends with rarities such as jesters, diviners, and merchants.

sentences.chinese: 惟漢繼五帝末流，接三代絕業。
sentences.literal: Only Han continues the Five Thearchs' late current, links to the three ages' cut-off enterprise.
sentences.idiomatic: Han alone carries the tail of the Five Thearchs and picks up the broken thread of the three dynasties.

sentences.chinese: 周道既廢，秦撥去古文，焚滅《詩》、《書》，故明堂、石室、金鐀、玉版圖籍散亂。
sentences.literal: Zhou's way was already cast aside; Qin cast away ancient script, burned and destroyed the Odes and Documents; therefore Bright Hall, stone chambers, metal chests, and jade-tablet charts and records were scattered in disorder.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhou's order was gone; Qin purged old texts and burned the classics, so palace archives in stone vaults and metal coffers fell into chaos.

sentences.chinese: 漢興，蕭何次律令，韓信申軍法，張蒼為章程，叔孫通定禮儀，則文學彬彬稍進，《詩》、《書》往往間出。
sentences.literal: When Han rose, Xiao He ordered statutes and ordinances, Han Xin extended military law, Zhang Cang made regulations and models, Shu Sun Tong fixed ritual and ceremony; then letters and learning gradually advanced, the Odes and Documents often emerged in patches.
sentences.idiomatic: Under Han, Xiao He codified law, Han Xin the military codes, Zhang Cang the bureaucratic rules, Shu Sun Tong court ritual—learning revived and fragments of the Odes and Documents resurfaced.

sentences.chinese: 自曹參薦蓋公言黃、老，而賈誼、韓錯明申、朝，公孫弘以儒顯，百年之間，天下遺文古事靡不畢集。
sentences.literal: From Cao Shen recommending Master Gai who spoke of Huang and Lao, while Jia Yi and Han Cuo (i.e. Chao Cuo) clarified Shen and Shang, Gongsun Hong by Ru became eminent—within a hundred years, all under heaven's stray writings and old affairs none were not fully gathered.
sentences.idiomatic: Cao Shen's patronage of Huang-Lao, Jia Yi and Chao Cuo's Legalism, Gongsun Hong's Confucian rise—within a century every scrap of old text and story was being gathered.

sentences.chinese: 太史公仍父子相繼籑其職，曰：「於戲！
sentences.literal: The grand scribe continuing father and son in succession compiling this office, said, "Alas!
sentences.idiomatic: The grand scribe, father and son in this same office, declared: "Alas!

sentences.chinese: 余維先人嘗掌斯事，顯於唐、虞；
sentences.literal: I consider how my forbears once grasped this affair, were eminent in Tang and Yu;
sentences.idiomatic: My ancestors held this charge under Yao and Shun;

sentences.chinese: 至於周，復典之。
sentences.literal: down to Zhou, again they presided over it.
sentences.idiomatic: in Zhou they held it again.

sentences.chinese: 故司馬氏世主天宮，至於余乎，欽念哉！」
sentences.literal: Therefore the Sima clan for generations presided over heaven's palace—down to me! Reverently heed this!"
sentences.idiomatic: So the Simas have watched heaven's offices for generations—down to me. Remember!"

sentences.chinese: 網羅天下放失舊聞，王跡所興，原始察終，見盛觀衰，論考之行事，略三代，錄秦、漢，上記軒轅，下至於茲，著十二本紀；
sentences.literal: He netted all under heaven's scattered and lost old hearsay, where kingly traces arose, sought beginnings and examined ends, saw flourishing and observed decline, discussed and examined them in deeds and conduct, summarized the three ages, recorded Qin and Han, upward recorded Xuanyuan, downward reached to here, and wrote twelve basic annals;
sentences.idiomatic: He gathered lost lore, traced where kingship rose and fell from the Yellow Thearch through three ages, Qin, and Han to the present, and set it down in twelve basic annals;

sentences.chinese: 既科條之矣，並時異世，年差不明，作十表；
sentences.literal: having already itemized them, concurrent times and different ages, years and differences unclear, he made ten tables;
sentences.idiomatic: then, because parallel ages were hard to align by year alone, he added ten tables;

sentences.chinese: 禮樂損益，律歷改易，兵權、山川、鬼神，天人之際，承敝通變，作八書；
sentences.literal: rites and music's increase and decrease, pitch-pipes and calendar's changes, military authority, mountains and rivers, ghosts and spirits, heaven and man's juncture, inheriting decay and penetrating change—he made eight treatises;
sentences.idiomatic: for ritual, music, law, calendar, war, rivers, spirits, and heaven's relation to man—eight treatises on change through decay and renewal;

sentences.chinese: 二十八宿環北辰，三十輻共一轂，運行無窮，輔弼股肱之臣配焉，忠信行道以奉主上，作三十世家；
sentences.literal: the twenty-eight lodges ring the north pole; thirty spokes share one hub; turning and moving without end—supporting thigh and arm ministers match them, loyally and faithfully practicing the way to serve the lord above—he made thirty hereditary houses;
sentences.idiomatic: like spokes round the hub he paired loyal ministers who bore the throne on their backs—thirty hereditary houses;

sentences.chinese: 扶義俶儻，不令己失時，立功名於天下，作七十列傳：凡百三十篇，五十二萬六千五百字，為《太史公書》。
sentences.literal: upholding righteousness and standing apart, not letting themselves miss the moment, establishing merit and fame within all under heaven—he made seventy ranked biographies: in all one hundred thirty sections, five hundred twenty-six thousand five hundred graphs, making the Grand Scribe's Documents.
sentences.idiomatic: and for men who seized the hour and won fame in the world—seventy biographies: one hundred thirty chapters, 526,500 characters, the book called the Grand Scribe's Documents.

sentences.chinese: 序略，以拾遺補闕，成一家言，協《六經》異傳，齊百家雜語，臧之名山，副在京師，以俟後聖君子。
sentences.literal: Summaries and outlines, to pick up omissions and mend gaps, complete one family's words, harmonize the Six Classics' divergent transmissions, align the hundred schools' mixed sayings, store them on a famous mountain, duplicate in the capital, to await later sage gentlemen.
sentences.idiomatic: Prefaces fill gaps, forge a single voice, reconcile variant traditions of the six classics and hundred schools, lodge a copy on a sacred peak and another in the capital, and wait for a later sage.

sentences.chinese: 第七十，遷之自敘云爾。
sentences.literal: The seventieth—Qian's self-narration ends thus.
sentences.idiomatic: The seventieth scroll is Qian's own epilogue.

sentences.chinese: 而十篇缺，有錄無書。
sentences.literal: Yet ten sections are missing—there are registers but no books.
sentences.idiomatic: Ten chapters survive only as titles—the text was lost.

sentences.chinese: 遷既被刑之後，為中書令，尊寵任職。
sentences.literal: After Qian had suffered mutilation, he became palace secretary, honored and favored in office.
sentences.idiomatic: After castration Qian became palace secretary, favored at court.

sentences.chinese: 故人益州刺史任安予遷書，責以古賢臣之義。
sentences.literal: His old friend Regional Inspector of Yi Ren An sent Qian a letter, reproaching him by the duty of ancient worthy ministers.
sentences.idiomatic: His old friend Ren An, governor of Yi province, wrote urging him to live up to the old standard of loyal ministers.

sentences.chinese: 遷報之曰：
sentences.literal: Qian replied to him, saying:
sentences.idiomatic: Qian answered:

sentences.chinese: 少卿足下：曩者辱賜書，教以慎於接物，推賢進士為務。
sentences.literal: Sir Shaoqing: formerly you deigned to send a letter, teaching me to be careful in dealing with others and to make recommending the worthy and advancing knights my task.
sentences.idiomatic: Sir Shaoqing: you did me honor with your letter, urging care in dealing with men and making the promotion of worth my duty.

sentences.chinese: 意氣勤勤懇懇，若望僕不相師用，而流俗人之言。
sentences.literal: Your intent was diligent and earnest, as if resenting that your servant would not take you as teacher and employ you, and instead followed vulgar men's words.
sentences.idiomatic: You pressed your case as though I refused your counsel and heeded only the gossip of the crowd.

sentences.chinese: 僕非敢如是也。
sentences.literal: Your servant does not dare to be thus.
sentences.idiomatic: I would not dare that.

sentences.chinese: 雖罷駑，亦嘗側聞長者遺風矣。
sentences.literal: Although I am a worn-out nag, I have also once overheard the surviving airs of elders.
sentences.idiomatic: Though I am only a broken nag, I have caught whispers of how the old gentlemen behaved.

sentences.chinese: 顧自以為身殘處穢，動而見尤，欲益反損，是以抑鬱而無誰語。
sentences.literal: Only I consider myself mutilated in body and dwelling in filth; moving, I meet blame; wishing to help, I instead harm—therefore I am depressed and have no one to speak to.
sentences.idiomatic: But I am a mutilated man in a foul station: every move draws blame, every try to help backfires, so I choke in silence with no one to hear me.

sentences.chinese: 諺曰：「誰為為之，孰令聽之？」
sentences.literal: The proverb says, "Who will do it for him—who will order it heard?"
sentences.idiomatic: The proverb asks, "Who would speak for me—and who would listen?"

sentences.chinese: 蓋鍾子期死，伯牙終身不復鼓琴。
sentences.literal: When Zhong Ziqi died, Bo Ya all his life never again plucked the zither.
sentences.idiomatic: When Zhong Ziqi died, Bo Ya never touched his strings again.

sentences.chinese: 何則？
sentences.literal: Why was it?
sentences.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.chinese: 士為知已用，女為說己容。
sentences.literal: A knight acts for one who knows him; a woman adorns herself for one who delights in her.
sentences.idiomatic: A man gives his strength to the friend who knows him; a woman paints herself for the eye that loves her.

sentences.chinese: 若僕大質已虧缺，雖材懷隨、行，行若由、夷，終不可以為榮，適足以發笑而自點耳。
sentences.literal: As for your servant, my great substance is already marred and lacking; though my talent held Sui and He (the pearl and jade), my conduct were like Xu You and Bo Yi—in the end it cannot be taken as glory, it is quite enough to draw laughter and smirch myself.
sentences.idiomatic: My substance is ruined: even with gifts like the Sui pearl and He's jade in talent, and the purity of Xu You and Bo Yi in conduct, I could not wear them as honor—only as a joke and a stain.

sentences.chinese: 書辭宜答，會東從上來，又迫賤事，相見日淺，卒卒無須臾之間得竭指意。
sentences.literal: The letter's words ought to be answered, but it happened I followed the sovereign east and came back, again was pressed by mean tasks, the days we met were shallow, hastily without a moment's space to exhaust my intent.
sentences.idiomatic: I owed you a reply, but I was trailing the emperor east, then swamped with petty duties; our meetings were brief and I never had a moment to lay out my mind.

sentences.chinese: 今少卿抱不測之罪，涉旬月，迫季冬，僕又薄從上上雍，恐卒然不可諱。
sentences.literal: Now Sir Shaoqing bears an unfathomable crime; it has crossed months; it presses the end of winter; your servant moreover is thinly following the sovereign up to Yong—I fear suddenly the unavoidable will come.
sentences.idiomatic: Now you face a capital charge; months have passed and deep winter nears; I am to follow the sovereign to Yong—any day the worst may happen.

sentences.chinese: 是僕終已不得舒憤懣以曉左右，則長逝者魂魄私恨無窮。
sentences.literal: In this case your servant in the end cannot unburden resentment and vexation to make clear to those about you, then the long-gone one's ghost will harbor private regret without end.
sentences.idiomatic: If I never unburden my heart to you, your spirit in death would nurse endless regret.

sentences.chinese: 請略陳固陋。
sentences.literal: I beg to set forth briefly my obstinate ignorance.
sentences.idiomatic: Let me state my crude thoughts.

sentences.chinese: 闕然不報，幸勿過。
sentences.literal: Having been remiss and not answered, I beg you not to fault me.
sentences.idiomatic: Forgive my long silence.

sentences.chinese: 僕聞之：修身者，智之府也；
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard: he who cultivates the person is wisdom's treasury;
sentences.idiomatic: I have heard that self-cultivation is the storehouse of wisdom;

sentences.chinese: 愛施者，仁之端也；
sentences.literal: he who loves and gives is benevolence's beginning;
sentences.idiomatic: that charity is the sprout of benevolence;
